contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately.

authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — candidate authoring assistant; human review required

items.prompt: A design review covers Strictly increasing input after a long decreasing frontier. Explain why one iteration may pop many entries without making total time quadratic. Which statement gives the decisive justification?
constraints: Use the exact output, strictness, scan direction, and tie semantics stated in the prompt.
Do not classify the algorithm from a variable name or one intermediate snapshot.

options.text: Those entries cannot be popped again if each is pushed once and never reinserted.
A nested while loop always implies O(n²).
A loop nested inside the scan makes the total cost quadratic regardless of how many times each entry can actually be removed.
The maximum stack size multiplied by the input length is always the exact running time, independent of the executed operations.

feedback.reason: Those entries cannot be popped again if each is pushed once and never reinserted. This is the decisive boundary for the stated output and retained-state semantics.
feedback.details: The item is decided by aggregate operation counts versus work attached to each transition, not by a surface feature. Those entries cannot be popped again if each is pushed once and never reinserted. The tempting claim—A nested while loop always implies O(n²).—fails because it does not establish the semantic invariant that makes removal or retention safe. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_false_heuristic: This is the targeted misconception. Those entries cannot be popped again if each is pushed once and never reinserted. The quoted clue does not establish that invariant or justify the removal rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.surface_feature_classifier: This relies on a surface feature instead of the contract. The correct decision follows from aggregate operation counts versus work attached to each transition; payload type or implementation shape alone is not diagnostic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unjustified_alternative_rule: This changes the representation or transition without proving that it preserves the requested result. Those entries cannot be popped again if each is pushed once and never reinserted.

items.prompt: Order the aggregate-accounting steps for a complete monotonic-stack trace.
constraints: Order the steps by semantic dependency, not by the order in which they are listed.
The canonical scoring contract awards preserved adjacent relations.

elements.text: Charge at most one later pop to that same occurrence.
Add the O(1) outer-scan work performed for each input occurrence.
Bound total work by O(n) scan steps plus at most n pushes and n pops.
Give every input occurrence a stable identity.
Count one push when each occurrence first becomes a candidate.

feedback.reason: The sequence follows the dependency expressed by the decisive boundary: Aggregate transitions are bounded by pushes plus pops, not by the worst inner-loop length per iteration.
feedback.details: Start from the output contract and the meaning of each retained entry. Aggregate transitions are bounded by pushes plus pops, not by the worst inner-loop length per iteration. That mechanism explains both the legal transition and the point at which an answer or pending state becomes final. The misconception “Sum the maximum possible pops independently for every outer iteration.” confuses an implementation clue with a correctness condition. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately. Reversing or separating a required adjacent relation either uses state before it is valid, loses provenance before writing an answer, or mutates the frontier before the invariant has been restored.

items.prompt: A next-greater scan processes n items. Each item is pushed once, popped at most once, and every comparison, push, pop, and answer write is O(1). What is the total time complexity?
constraints: Count the operations stated in the prompt; do not infer a complexity from the stack label alone.
Treat arithmetic, comparison, push, pop, and one answer write as O(1) unless the prompt explicitly attaches larger work.

feedback.reason: At most 2n stack transitions plus O(n) scan work yields O(n).
feedback.details: The stack category follows from the proof obligation. At most 2n stack transitions plus O(n) scan work yields O(n). Names, indices, comparisons, loop shape, or one trace snapshot cannot replace that proof. In this case, the stated misconception—One iteration with n pops makes total O(n²).—would permit transitions that are not justified by the requested result. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately. The derivation counts executed work over the entire input and keeps peak retained state separate from total transition count.

constraints: Use the exact output, strictness, scan direction, and tie semantics stated in the prompt.

options.text: The maximum stack size multiplied by the input length is always the exact running time, independent of the executed operations.
Count total pushes and irreversible pops over the entire run.
Analyze each loop iteration in isolation.
A loop nested inside the scan makes the total cost quadratic regardless of how many times each entry can actually be removed.

feedback.reason: Count total pushes and irreversible pops over the entire run. This is the decisive boundary for the stated output and retained-state semantics.
feedback.details: Apply the contract before choosing the representation. Count total pushes and irreversible pops over the entire run. This preserves the relevant state and prevents premature deletion or unnecessary ordering. The alternative heuristic—Analyze each loop iteration in isolation.—is too broad and would classify unrelated stack algorithms the same way. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately.

items.prompt: Select all valid charging assignments for the constant work in a monotonic-stack pop loop.
constraints: Select all and only the statements that remain valid under the exact output and tie policy.
Each selected statement must be justified by retained-state semantics or a correctness/cost argument.

options.text: Ignore work performed during pops because only pushes count toward amortized analysis.
Charge each pop to the unique candidate entry that is removed.
Use the fact that one candidate can be removed at most once after being pushed once.
Include the constant comparison and answer-write work associated with that removal in the same charge.
Charge n units to every outer-loop iteration because the inner loop could be long in isolation.

feedback.reason: The accepted set jointly establishes the decisive boundary: Charge each pop and its constant work to the unique entry being removed.
feedback.details: The item is decided by aggregate operation counts versus work attached to each transition, not by a surface feature. Charge each pop and its constant work to the unique entry being removed. The tempting claim—Charge every pop to every earlier outer iteration.—fails because it does not establish the semantic invariant that makes removal or retention safe. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately.

items.prompt: Order a potential-method proof using Φ = current stack size.
constraints: Order the steps by semantic dependency, not by the order in which they are listed.
The canonical scoring contract awards preserved adjacent relations.

elements.text: A push performs O(1) actual work and increases Φ by 1, storing one unit of potential.
A pop performs O(1) actual work and decreases Φ by 1, releasing stored potential to pay for the pop.
Combine actual work and potential change to bound each processed input by O(1) amortized stack-transition cost.
Sum over n inputs and use nonnegative initial/final potential to obtain O(n) total transition work.
Define the nonnegative potential Φ as the current number of retained entries.

feedback.reason: The sequence follows the dependency expressed by the decisive boundary: Push increases potential; pop releases stored potential that pays for its work.
feedback.details: Start from the output contract and the meaning of each retained entry. Push increases potential; pop releases stored potential that pays for its work. That mechanism explains both the legal transition and the point at which an answer or pending state becomes final. The misconception “Potential may become negative to hide repeated work.” confuses an implementation clue with a correctness condition. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately. items.prompt: An algorithm performs k = 4 independent directional boundary passes. Each pass scans n items and uses amortized O(1) stack-transition work per item. What is the simplified total time complexity?
constraints: Count the operations stated in the prompt; do not infer a complexity from the stack label alone.
Treat arithmetic, comparison, push, pop, and one answer write as O(1) unless the prompt explicitly attaches larger work.

feedback.reason: A fixed product constant does not change asymptotic linearity; growing k must remain explicit.
feedback.details: Apply the contract before choosing the representation. A fixed product constant does not change asymptotic linearity; growing k must remain explicit. This preserves the relevant state and prevents premature deletion or unnecessary ordering. The alternative heuristic—All repeated passes can be discarded from the cost model.—is too broad and would classify unrelated stack algorithms the same way. Transfer the aggregate or potential proof only when each entry has a bounded number of constant-work transitions; count any attached scan, copy, or nonconstant work separately. The derivation counts executed work over the entire input and keeps peak retained state separate from total transition count.
